name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Dark concentric spots on lower leaves
Yellowing and premature leaf drop
Reduced fruit size and yield
immediate_action: Remove infected leaves immediately
Improve airflow by pruning
Avoid overhead irrigation

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Severe upward leaf curling and distortion
Yellowing (chlorosis) of leaf margins
Stunted growth; poor flower and fruit set
immediate_action: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
Control whitefly vectors aggressively
Isolate surrounding healthy plants
chemical_solution: Systemic insecticides (e.g., Imidacloprid) for vector control
preventive_measures: Plant during cooler periods when whitefly activity is low

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on leaves turning brown/black
White, fuzzy growth (spores) on the underside of leaves in humid conditions
Rotting tubers with reddish-brown dry decay
immediate_action: Apply fungicide immediately at first sign
Destroy severely infected foliage (haulms)
Avoid harvesting tubers until two weeks after chemical haulm destruction
chemical_solution: Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil for protectant spray
Systemic fungicides (e.g., Propamocarb) for curative treatment
preventive_measures: Hilling/Earthing up to prevent spore washdown to tubers
Improve drainage and avoid excessive irrigation

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Rough, corky, or pitted lesions on tuber skin
Reduced market value, cosmetic damage only
Most severe in dry soil conditions during tuber set
immediate_action: No immediate cure for current crop, focus on mitigation
Increase soil moisture in the tuberization zone
Do not save infected tubers for planting next season
organic_solution: Apply sulfur to reduce soil pH (long-term goal)
Use green manure crops in rotation
preventive_measures: Ensure adequate and consistent soil moisture during tuber set

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Bright yellow/orange pustules on leaves arranged in stripes or bands
Pustules break open, releasing yellow powdery spores
Reduced photosynthesis leading to shriveled grain and yield loss
immediate_action: Apply a systemic fungicide immediately if threshold is met
Scout surrounding fields for source of inoculum
Increase potassium fertilizer application
organic_solution: Limited effective organic options; focus on resistant varieties
preventive_measures: Plant resistant or tolerant wheat varieties
Avoid planting overly early (high risk for early infection)
Destroy volunteer wheat and grassy weeds

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Premature bleaching/blighting of individual or entire spikelets on the head
Pinkish or orange growth on infected spikelets in humid conditions
Produces mycotoxin (DON) which contaminates grain
immediate_action: Apply fungicide during the early flowering (anthesis) stage (most critical window)
If grain is harvested, segregate affected fields
organic_solution: Biological control agents (e.g., Clonostachys rosea) as seed treatment
chemical_solution: Triazole fungicides (e.g., Metconazole, Tebuconazole) at flowering
preventive_measures: Rotate wheat with non-cereal crops (corn is a major host, so avoid rotation after corn)

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Upward curling and thickening of older leaves
Formation of cup-shaped, dark-green leafy outgrowths (enations) on the underside of leaves
Severe stunting and reduced boll size/number
immediate_action: Remove and destroy heavily diseased plants
Aggressive control of the whitefly vector
Apply insecticides immediately
organic_solution: Neem oil or horticultural oil for whitefly suppression
Cultural control: water and nutrient management
chemical_solution: Systemic neonicotinoid insecticides (seed treatment and foliar)

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Angular, water-soaked spots on leaves (limited by veins)
Black lesions on stems (Black Arm symptom)
Water-soaked spots on bolls turning black and rotting
immediate_action: Apply Copper-based bactericide immediately
Prune affected branches during dry conditions
Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet
chemical_solution: Copper hydroxide or Copper oxychloride sprays
preventive_measures: Deep plowing to bury infested trash

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Diamond-shaped lesions with grayish-white centers and dark borders on leaves
Lesions on the neck (neck blast) causing the panicle to break
Blighted panicles with incomplete grain filling
immediate_action: Apply a systemic fungicide immediately (especially targeting the neck blast stage)
Reduce excessive nitrogen fertilization
Drain the field temporarily
organic_solution: Silicon-containing fertilizers to enhance host resistance
preventive_measures: Optimal nitrogen management (avoiding too much too early)

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Water-soaked lesions on the leaf sheath, usually near the water line
Lesions grow vertically and turn grayish-white with purple/brown margins
White fungal bodies (sclerotia) produced on the sheath surface
immediate_action: Apply fungicide targeting the base of the plant (sheath)
Drain the field to lower humidity near the base
Reduce plant density to improve air circulation
organic_solution: Antagonistic fungi (e.g., Trichoderma viride) applied to soil
preventive_measures: Use moderate levels of nitrogen fertilizer
Proper spacing of plants (avoiding dense canopy)

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Small, circular spots with dark borders and tan/gray centers on leaves
Tiny black specks (pycnidia) visible in the center of spots
Starts on the oldest leaves and moves up
immediate_action: Remove and destroy lowest infected leaves
Stake plants to improve air circulation
Apply fungicide to protect new growth
preventive_measures: Clean up all plant debris at the end of the season

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Small, dark, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn brown/black and greasy
Scab-like, raised spots on fruit
Lesions on stems and petioles
immediate_action: Discontinue overhead irrigation
Prune and destroy infected tissue during dry weather
Apply Copper-based sprays

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Small, dark brown spots on older leaves
Spots enlarge and form concentric rings (target pattern)
Leaf tissue around spots turns yellow
immediate_action: Apply protective fungicide (Chlorothalonil) immediately
Maintain adequate irrigation to reduce plant stress
preventive_measures: Maintain good plant nutrition, especially nitrogen and potassium
Control weeds that can host the fungus

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Mottling, crinkling, or mosaic pattern on leaves
Severe stunting and leaf drop (necrotic strains)
Transmitted by aphids
immediate_action: Roguing (removal and destruction) of symptomatic plants
Control aphid vectors
Use mineral oil sprays to deter aphids
organic_solution: Neem oil or horticultural oil for aphid control
preventive_measures: Plant barrier crops around the field to deter aphids
Timely harvest to avoid late-season spread

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Small, circular, raised orange-brown pustules, scattered randomly on upper leaf surface
Pustules rupture to release cinnamon-brown spores
Reduced kernel weight and yield
immediate_action: Apply a foliar fungicide if the disease severity justifies the cost (e.g., >5% severity)
Focus on protecting the flag leaf (most important for yield)

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Irregular, dark brown lesions on lower leaves
Lesions enlarge and turn light gray/tan with tiny black specks (pycnidia) in the center
Disease spreads up the plant canopy
immediate_action: Apply fungicide (usually at the flag leaf stage) to protect the top leaves
Fungicide timing is critical for control
organic_solution: Focus on cultural practices: crop rotation, burying residue
preventive_measures: Tillage to bury wheat stubble (primary source of inoculum)

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Yellowing (chlorosis) and wilting of one side of a leaf or the entire plant
Dark brown to black discoloration in the vascular tissues (stems)
Premature defoliation and shedding of squares/bolls
immediate_action: No chemical cure; focus on symptom management
Maintain adequate water and balanced fertility to reduce plant stress
preventive_measures: Crop rotation with non-host crops (e.g., small grains)

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Sudden, permanent wilting of the plant
Brown vascular discoloration (darker than Verticillium) in the lower stem and taproot
Often occurs in conjunction with root-knot nematode damage
immediate_action: Plant destruction to prevent disease spread
Soil testing for nematode presence
organic_solution: Use of resistant varieties and nematode control
preventive_measures: Plant a non-host rotation crop to suppress the fungus and nematodes
Deep tillage to reduce soil inoculum

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Water-soaked stripes on leaf margins near the tip (Kresek symptom in seedlings: wilting of entire plant)
Lesions turn yellow, then grayish-white, often with a wavy margin
Bacterial ooze visible as yellowish beads in the morning
immediate_action: Drain the field water immediately
Reduce nitrogen fertilizer application
Stop clipping seedlings before transplanting
organic_solution: Application of antagonistic bacteria (e.g., Pseudomonas fluorescens)
chemical_solution: Copper compounds or Streptomycin (in some regions/limited use)
preventive_measures: Treat seeds with hot water or chemicals
Avoid damage to leaves and maintain low water levels

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Oval or circular dark brown spots with a pale yellow halo on leaves and glumes
Spots may merge to form large blotches
High incidence of infection on grains (poor quality, low yield)
immediate_action: Apply foliar fungicide if severity is high
Apply potassium and zinc fertilizers (nutrient deficiency is key predisposing factor)
organic_solution: Use of silicon amendments in soil
preventive_measures: Use healthy seeds; seed treatment with fungicide

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Yellowing of lower leaves, often starting on one side of the plant or leaf
Wilting of the plant during the day, recovering at night
Dark brown streaking in the vascular tissue of the lower stem
immediate_action: No chemical control available; remove infected plants
Do not save seeds from infected fruit
organic_solution: Biofungicides (e.g., Trichoderma harzianum) in soil pre-planting
preventive_measures: Lower soil temperature through heavy mulching

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Yellowing of lower leaves, often starting as V-shaped lesions on leaf tips
Less severe wilting than Fusarium
Light brown discoloration in the vascular tissue
immediate_action: No chemical control; remove infected plants
Minimize plant stress (water and temperature)
preventive_measures: Crop rotation with non-host crops (e.g., corn, wheat)

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Stunted growth with rolling/yellowing of upper leaves (Blackleg)
Black, slimy decay at the base of the stem
Tubers develop a foul-smelling, mushy soft rot in storage
immediate_action: Remove and destroy infected plants and neighboring plants
Improve field drainage immediately
Avoid overhead irrigation in infected areas
organic_solution: Focus on cultural control and sanitation
chemical_solution: Seed piece treatment with streptomycin or copper formulations
preventive_measures: Avoid injuring tubers during harvest and handling

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Brownish-black cankers on young sprouts and stems below ground
Aerial tubers and leaf rolling (if stems are girdled)
Hard, black masses (scurf) on the surface of mature tubers
immediate_action: No action during the season for canker; focus on prevention next year
Maintain adequate soil moisture during the emergence period
preventive_measures: Plant late to avoid cold, wet conditions
Avoid excessive irrigation after tuber initiation

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Premature whitening/bleaching of entire heads (whiteheads) in patches
Severely stunted growth
Blackening of roots and the base of the stem (crown)
immediate_action: No in-season chemical control available
Avoid harvesting from infected patches to prevent spreading soil inoculum
organic_solution: Increase soil manganese and ammonia-based nitrogen
preventive_measures: Strict rotation with non-cereal crops (3+ years)
Maintain soil pH at or below 6.0

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Heads emerge as a mass of black, powdery spores (smut dust)
Spores are blown away, leaving a bare central stalk (rachis)
Infected heads are visible shortly after heading
immediate_action: Rogue (remove) infected heads before spores disperse
No chemical control is effective after emergence
organic_solution: Hot water treatment of seed (difficult, high risk)

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Small, dark, circular spots on older leaves that enlarge and develop concentric rings
Severe premature defoliation, especially under potassium deficiency
Spots may also occur on bolls
immediate_action: Apply a foliar fungicide if defoliation is severe
Apply foliar potassium nitrate to alleviate nutrient stress
preventive_measures: Bury infected crop residue after harvest

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Distinct, large spots with dark brown concentric rings (target pattern)
Starts on lower canopy leaves and moves up
Causes premature defoliation and yield loss
immediate_action: Apply a fungicide (especially important for mid-to-late season growth)
Improve air circulation within the canopy
chemical_solution: Fungicides from the Strobilurin group (e.g., Azoxystrobin)

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Stunting of plants, yellow-orange discoloration of younger leaves
Leaves may show mild mottling
Transmitted by the green leafhopper insect vector
immediate_action: Rouge and destroy infected plants
Aggressive control of the green leafhopper (GLH) vector
Drain water to discourage GLH
organic_solution: Neem oil spray for GLH control
Early planting to avoid high GLH populations
chemical_solution: Systemic insecticides (e.g., Neonicotinoids) for GLH control
preventive_measures: Synchronous planting (planting all fields in the area at the same time)
Control volunteer rice plants and weeds

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Individual rice grains transform into velvety, greenish-black spore balls (false smut balls)
Balls rupture to release powdery spores
Usually affects only a few grains per panicle
immediate_action: Remove smut balls manually before they burst (field sanitation)
Reduce high nitrogen application
chemical_solution: Fungicides (e.g., Propiconazole) applied at the booting stage
preventive_measures: Use varieties that head out uniformly

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Brown, water-soaked spots on fruit that develop dark, concentric bands (like a buckeye)
Typically found on fruit touching the soil surface
Soft, mushy rot
immediate_action: Remove infected fruit and foliage
Apply protective fungicide
Mulch to prevent fruit contact with soil
preventive_measures: Staking and pruning to keep fruit off the ground

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Water-soaked spot on the blossom end of the fruit (opposite the stem)
Spot enlarges, turns dark brown/black, leathery and sunken
Not a pathogen, but a physiological disorder (calcium deficiency)
immediate_action: Apply calcium chloride or calcium nitrate foliar spray immediately
Ensure consistent, even watering
organic_solution: Apply calcium-rich amendments (e.g., gypsum) to the soil
preventive_measures: Avoid high nitrogen fertilizer late in the season

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Mottling, mild crinkling, and light-green mosaic patterns on leaves
Plants are generally stunted but may still produce tubers
Spread by aphids and contact
immediate_action: Rouge infected plants and volunteer potatoes
Control aphid vectors
organic_solution: Mineral oil sprays to deter aphids
preventive_measures: Eliminate all volunteer potatoes and weeds (hosts)

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Light brown to silvery patches on the tuber skin, visible when wet
Patches darken over time, especially in storage
Causes water loss and shriveling of tubers in storage
immediate_action: No in-season field control; focus on storage conditions
Harvesting early may limit severity
chemical_solution: Post-harvest fungicide treatment on tubers (e.g., Thiabendazole)
preventive_measures: Ensure proper ventilation and lower temperatures (below 4°C) in storage

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Infected kernels are replaced by a mass of black spores (bunt balls)
Heads appear slightly blue-green and remain upright instead of drooping
Infected grain releases a foul, fishy odor when crushed
immediate_action: Rogue (remove) infected heads (only practical for light infection)
Do not save seed from infected fields
organic_solution: Hot water seed treatment (difficult and high risk)
chemical_solution: Systemic fungicide seed treatments (mandatory for high-risk areas)
preventive_measures: Clean all equipment that contacts seed or grain

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Stunting of plants, reduced tillering
Leaf tips turn yellow, red, or purple, especially older leaves
Poor grain fill; transmitted by aphids
immediate_action: No direct chemical control for the virus
Control aphid vectors if population is high
organic_solution: Neem oil/horticultural oil for aphid control
chemical_solution: Seed treatment with systemic insecticide to protect seedlings from aphids
Foliar insecticide for severe aphid infestations
preventive_measures: Delayed planting to avoid peak aphid migration
Control volunteer cereals and grassy weeds

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: White fungal patches on the underside of leaves
Upper surface shows yellowish, angular spots (limited by leaf veins)
Premature defoliation
immediate_action: Apply a sulfur-based fungicide
Improve air movement in the canopy

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Dark, sunken lesions on seedlings (damping-off)
Reddish-brown spots on bolls that turn black and sunken
Pinkish-orange spore masses in the center of boll lesions when moist
immediate_action: Remove and destroy infected bolls and plants
Improve drainage and reduce canopy humidity
organic_solution: Copper sprays (limited efficacy on mature bolls)
preventive_measures: Rotate with non-host crops (e.g., corn, wheat)
Deep plowing to bury infested residue

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Yellowing of the basal part of the youngest leaves
Brownish-bronze spots appear on older leaves
Stunting and delayed maturity (physiological disorder, not pathogen)
immediate_action: Spray 0.5% Zinc sulfate solution (two applications)
Drain field and allow soil to dry out
organic_solution: Foliar application of chelated zinc products
preventive_measures: Apply Zinc sulfate before transplanting in deficient soils

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Severe stunting and excessive tillering (grassy appearance)
Leaves are shorter, narrower, and often yellowish-green
Transmitted by the brown planthopper (BPH) vector
immediate_action: Rouge and destroy infected plants
Aggressive control of the BPH vector
Monitor BPH population with yellow sticky traps
chemical_solution: Systemic insecticides (e.g., Buprofezin, Imidacloprid) for BPH

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Small, dark, circular spots on leaves that develop target-like concentric rings
Causes defoliation and sunscald on fruit
Infected fruit has dark, slightly sunken, firm lesions
immediate_action: Apply protective fungicide (Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb)
Improve air circulation

name: Tomato
common_symptoms: Vibrant green foliage
Strong, upright growth habit
Consistent fruit setting and sizing
immediate_action: Routine monitoring (scouting)
Ensure balanced NPK and adequate watering
preventive_measures: Maintain mulch layer to conserve moisture
Fertilize based on soil test results

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Mines (tunnels) within leaf tissue, especially near the veins
Tunnels and larvae visible inside tubers near the eyes
Frass (excrement) accumulation around tunnel entrance
immediate_action: Control flying adult moths with pheromone traps
Apply insecticides to foliage
Destroy infested tubers
organic_solution: Pheromone traps for mass trapping/mating disruption
preventive_measures: Effective hilling (earthing up) to protect tubers
Harvest promptly and deep-bury infested residue
Store tubers under cool, dark conditions

name: Potato
common_symptoms: Lush, full, green canopy
Uniform plant emergence
No visible lesions or wilting
immediate_action: Continue routine scouting for pests and disease
Monitor soil moisture for consistent supply
preventive_measures: Maintain hilling around the base of plants

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Erect, vibrant green leaves and tillers
Uniform stand height and maturity
Clean, well-filled heads
immediate_action: Continue monitoring; especially watch for rust diseases near flag leaf emergence
chemical_solution: No application needed (unless for prophylactic use based on risk models)
preventive_measures: Ensure final top-dressing of nitrogen is correct
Monitor weather conditions (moisture/temperature) for disease forecasting

name: Wheat
common_symptoms: Brown lesions on the glumes (chaff) that turn purplish-brown at the tip
Dark brown lesions on leaf nodes and sheaths
Black specks (pycnidia) in the center of lesions on leaves and glumes
immediate_action: Apply fungicide to protect the upper canopy
Fungicide timing should align with flag leaf and head emergence

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Dark green, well-developed leaves
Good square and boll retention
Healthy white roots
immediate_action: Continue insect pest management
Monitor water needs during boll filling stage
preventive_measures: Avoid late-season excessive nitrogen (to promote timely maturity)

name: Cotton
common_symptoms: Small, circular, yellowish spots on leaves and bolls
Spots develop dark borders and whitish-gray centers
Often appears during prolonged cool, wet periods
immediate_action: Apply a broad-spectrum fungicide (Chlorothalonil)
Avoid working in the field when plants are wet

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Uniform green color across the field
Strong tillering and panicle development
No signs of discoloration or wilting
immediate_action: Monitor water level and maintain optimal depth
Scout for Brown Planthopper (BPH) and Leaf Folder

name: Rice
common_symptoms: Irregular lesions on the flag leaf sheath that cover the emerging panicle
Panicle emergence is inhibited; panicle may be partially diseased or rotten
Brown to black lesions with irregular edges
immediate_action: Apply bactericide (e.g., Copper compounds)
Reduce N fertilizer application at the panicle emergence stage
preventive_measures: Control sheath mite and other insects that cause wounds
